channel.title: NYT > Books > Book Review
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: A Romance Novelist Finds Inspiration in Tension Around ‘Star Trek’
item.description: A 1967 correspondence led Cat Sebastian to imagine a contemporary scenario in which two sci-fi actors find more than screen chemistry.
item.dc:creator: Angelina Mazza
item.media:credit: Kannetha Brown for The New York Times
item.media:description: The political climate in Florida, including book bans, led Cat Sebastian to move her family to Connecticut.

item.title: Books Our Editors Loved This Week
item.description: Reading recommendations from critics and editors at The New York Times.

item.title: Dark and Stormy Thrillers by Stephen King, Ruth Ware and More
item.description: The best-selling author Lisa Unger recommends her favorite dark and stormy thrillers by Stephen King, Ruth Ware and more.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Unger

item.title: Book Review: ‘Kids, Wait Till You Hear This! A Memoir,’ by Liza Minnelli
item.description: “Kids, Wait Till You Hear This!” is a familiar reminder that growing up in showbiz can lead to awards and adulation, but also to heartache.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Jacobs
item.category: Kids, Wait Till You Hear This! (Book)
item.media:description: Liza Minnelli in 1972, the year she starred as Sally Bowles in “Cabaret.”

item.title: The 10 Best Books of 2025
item.description: The staff of The New York Times Book Review choose the year’s top fiction and nonfiction.
item.category: The Loneliness of Sonia and Sunny (Book)
There Is No Place for Us: Working and Homeless in America (Book)
Mother Emanuel: Two Centuries of Race, Resistance, and Forgiveness in One Charleston Church (Book)
A Marriage at Sea: A True Story of Love, Obsession, and Shipwreck (Book)
Wild Thing: A Life of Paul Gauguin (Book)

item.title: Histories of Native American Treaties and Anti-Chinese Violence Win Bancroft Prize
item.description: The award, one of the most prestigious in the field of American history, honors “scope, significance, depth of research and richness of interpretation.”
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler
item.category: Vested Interests: Trusteeship and Native Dispossession in the United States (Book)
John Doe Chinaman: A Forgotten History of Chinese Life Under American Racial Law (Book)

item.title: Stephen Koch, 84, Dies; Champion of a Belatedly Hailed Photographer
item.description: A writer and critic, Mr. Koch struggled for years to shepherd his friend Peter Hujar’s underappreciated, Bohemian-world artwork to posthumous glory.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter

item.title: Peter Schneider Dies at 85; His Novels Explored a Divided Germany
item.description: His best-known work, “The Wall Jumper,” proved prescient in its contention that the country would remain split even after reunification.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:description: Peter Schneider in 1994. He was considered one of the most important literary figures of his generation, in part because his life was so intertwined with the postwar German experience.

item.title: Jill Biden Will Publish a Memoir in June
item.description: In “View from the East Wing,” the former first lady will recount her time in the White House and share her views on the 2024 presidential race.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times

item.title: Dan Simmons, Genre-Leaping Author of ‘The Terror,’ Dies at 77
item.description: He moved easily and prolifically through science fiction, fantasy, horror, thrillers, crime and historical fiction. His book “The Terror” was made into a cable TV series.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:description: Dan Simmons in 1996. “He said from the beginning that he wanted to write what he wanted to write,” his wife, Karen Simmons, said, “and if the publisher didn’t want him to go in that direction, he’d switch publishers.”

item.title: Book Review: ‘In the Days of My Youth I Was Told What It Means to Be a Man,’ by Tom Junod
item.description: In an affecting new memoir, Tom Junod, a prizewinning magazine writer, grapples with unsettling discoveries about his larger-than-life dad.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Szalai
item.category: In the Days of My Youth I Was Told What It Means to Be a Man (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Golden Boy,’ by Patricia Finn
item.description: From his perch in Hawaii, the hero of Patricia Finn’s first novel, “The Golden Boy,” revisits his dark past in rural Ontario.
item.dc:creator: Beatriz Williams

item.title: Book Review: ‘Down Time,’ by Andrew Martin
item.description: In Andrew Martin’s keenly observed new novel, a group of friends navigate a society reshaped by the pandemic.
item.dc:creator: Ben Greenman

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Complex,’ by Karan Mahajan
item.description: Karan Mahajan’s new novel, “The Complex,” tracks the fortunes of a political family in a rapidly changing India.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Dee

item.title: Book Review: ‘Nonesuch,’ by Francis Spufford
item.description: “Nonesuch,” the new novel by Francis Spufford, conjures a plot laced with magic to change the course of history.
item.dc:creator: Louisa Hall

item.title: Greg Greeley, Former Amazon Executive, to Lead Simon & Schuster
item.description: Greg Greeley, who once ran Amazon’s books and media business, will succeed Jonathan Karp as chief executive at one of the largest book publishers in the U.S.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:description: Greg Greeley, whose 19-year tenure at Amazon included a stint running its books and media business, has succeeded Jonathan Karp as chief executive of Simon & Schuster.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Gunk,’ by Saba Sams
item.description: “Gunk,” a novel by Saba Sams, follows a woman through the trials and tenuous jobs of young adulthood.
item.dc:creator: Dwight Garner
item.media:description: Saba Sams was raised in Brighton, England, the setting of her debut novel, “Gunk.”

item.title: Book Review: ‘Whidbey,’ by T Kira Madden
item.description: In “Whidbey,” three women reckon with the aftermath of sexual assault.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Chidgey

item.title: He Wrote Judy Blume’s Life Story. She Won’t Talk About It.
item.description: Mark Oppenheimer had many conversations with his subject for his new book. Then the relationship took a turn.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Egan
item.media:credit: Jillian Freyer for The New York Times
item.media:description: “It was like doing another college degree, but you’re majoring in Judy Blume,” Mark Oppenheimer (here in his Connecticut home) says of his extensive research.

item.title: Tatjana Wood, Award-Winning Comic Book Colorist, Dies at 99
item.description: She was part of the acclaimed creative teams on comic book series for DC Comics, including Swamp Thing, which she called “Shvampy” in her German accent.
item.dc:creator: George Gene Gustines

item.title: Book Review: ‘Stories,’ by Helen Garner
item.description: A newly released collection of the Australian master’s short fiction shows her sympathy, her virtuosity and her ear.
item.dc:creator: Joumana Khatib
item.media:description: One of Australia’s most acclaimed writers, Garner creates work that is visceral, incandescent and unmistakably her own.

item.title: Jeremy Larner, 88, Dies; Wrote ‘The Candidate,’ a Political Film Classic
item.description: His Oscar-winning 1972 screenplay starred Robert Redford as an idealistic public interest lawyer making a run for the Senate.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: From 2014: Maya Angelou, Lyrical Witness of the Jim Crow South, Dies at 86
item.description: Her landmark book “I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings” was among the first 20th-century autobiographies of a Black woman to reach a wide readership.
item.dc:creator: Margalit Fox

item.title: The Avett Brothers’ Bassist on Writing a John Quincy Adams Book
item.description: Bob Crawford discusses the leap from stage to page and why his new book, “America’s Founding Son,” feels so relevant.
item.dc:creator: Gilbert Cruz, Sarah Diamond, Amy Pearl and Larissa Anderson
item.category: America's Founding Son: John Quincy Adams, from President to Political Maverick (Book)
item.media:credit: Illustration by Christoph Niemann; Photo: Jeremy M. Lange for The New York Times

item.title: From 2019: Toni Morrison, Towering Novelist of the Black Experience, Dies at 88
item.description: Ms. Morrison, who wrote “Beloved” and “Song of Solomon,” was the first African-American woman to win the Nobel in literature.
item.dc:creator: Margalit Fox

item.title: From 2010: Lucille Clifton, Poet Who Explored Black Lives, Dies at 73
item.description: A distinguished American poet, she examined the experience of being Black and female in the 20th century.
item.dc:creator: Margalit Fox
item.media:credit: Afro American Newspapers/Gado, via Getty Images

item.title: From 1967: Dorothy Parker, Literary Wit, Dies at Age 73
item.description: She enjoyed a lifelong reputation as a glittering, annihilating humorist. For her epitaph, she suggested, “Excuse My Dust.”
item.dc:creator: Alden Whitman
item.media:description: Dorothy Parker in 1941. Her writing often appeared in The New Yorker, starting with the magazine’s second issue, on Feb. 28, 1925.

item.title: From 1992: Audre Lorde, 58, a Poet, Memoirist and Lecturer, Dies
item.description: Her large body of work, which included poetry, essays and autobiography, reflected her hatred of racial and sexual prejudice.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: Audre Lorde in 1983. Six years later, her collection of essays, “A Burst of Light,” won an American Book Award.

item.title: From 1960: Zora Hurston, 57, Writer, Is Dead
item.description: Although her books, written in the dialect of the Deep South, established her as one of the foremost writers of Black folklore, she died in obscurity.
item.dc:creator: The Associated Press
item.media:description: Zora Neale Hurston in the late 1930s or early ’40s. Her eight books, and the numerous short stories and articles she published in magazines, were well received by critics.

item.title: From 2006: Oriana Fallaci, Incisive Italian Journalist, Is Dead at 77
item.description: An iconoclastic journalist, she was known for her war coverage and her aggressive, revealing interviews with the powerful.
item.dc:creator: Ian Fisher
item.media:description: Oriana Fallaci in the 1980s. A glamorous figure with an ever-present cigarette, she was once described in The Los Angeles Times as “the journalist to whom virtually no world figure would say no.”

item.title: From 1957: Gabriela Mistral, Poet, Is Dead; Won Nobel Prize for Literature
item.description: She was recognized in 1945 for three “Soñetos de la Muerte” (“Sonnets of Death”), which were first published in Chile in 1922.
item.dc:creator: Special to The New York Times
item.media:description: Gabriela Mistral, seated, in 1945, showing her Nobel Prize to the Swedish writer Hjalmar Gullberg, right.

item.title: From 1968: Helen Keller, 87, Dies; She Became Symbol of Courage
item.description: She overcame blindness and deafness, but insisted that there was nothing miraculous about her achievements.
item.dc:creator: Special to The New York Times

item.title: From 1975: Hannah Arendt, Political Scientist, Is Dead at 69
item.description: She caused controversy with books like “Eichmann in Jerusalem,” published in 1963, which grew out of her coverage of Adolf Eichmann’s trial for The New Yorker.
item.dc:creator: David Bird
item.media:credit: Fred Stein Archive/Archive Photos, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Hannah Arendt in 1944. She fled to the United States four years earlier, when the Nazis were pressing in on France.

item.title: Tom Junod Would Like to Tell You About His Father
item.description: A star writer from the heyday of magazines reveals the family secret behind his award-winning stories.
item.dc:creator: Steven Kurutz
item.media:credit: Mark Sommerfeld for The New York Times

item.title: New Nonfiction to Read This Spring
item.description: Memoirs from Liza Minnelli and Arsenio Hall; essays from David Sedaris and Jesmyn Ward; plus histories, true crime, biographies and more.

item.title: New Novels to Read This Spring
item.description: New novels from Tana French, Emma Straub, Ben Lerner, Solvej Balle, Shannon Chakraborty, Tom Perrotta, Elizabeth Strout — and plenty more.

item.title: New Chinese Picture Books
item.description: In “Little Monk Writes Rain,” “Yulu’s Linen” and “Lost in Peach Blossom Paradise,” spirited children meet Eastern visual traditions that have a life of their own.
item.dc:creator: Leonard S. Marcus
